posts.name: Jürgen Schmidt
posts.cooked:
2022 Château Pichon Longueville Comtesse de Lalande​

I have to admit I am stunned, as I have never tasted before such outstanding youth Bordeaux`s in my life. All were marked by an impressive density, balance, fruit concentration, polished tannins, freshness and drinking pleasure. And thankfully we don’t have to wait 50 years, so we are all still alive to enjoy these beauties. Wonderful to drink right now, maybe best in 5-10 years. In my view I see no real benefit of BIG aging. It doesn’t get any better.

It’s a blend of 78% Cabernet Sauvignon, 17% Merlot and 5% Cabernet Franc that spent 18 months in barriques (three months more than was formerly the case), two-thirds of which were new.

The bouquet is captivating and voluptous, black velvet all over. What a density and elegance with no end. The velvet texture is such sexy. Fair to say a big (!) wine and for me a touch too modern, I prefer 2022 Montrose instead. Anyway truely a 100/100 wine.

IMG_60443024×4032 2.51 MB

posts.name: Mark C Johnson
posts.cooked: What does that make Montrose. 11?

posts.name: Jürgen Schmidt
posts.cooked:
110/100.

The most famous German Bordeaux importer gave 100+

Of course, also a 100/100 wine, but the stilistic is different. More classic in a certain way, as 2022 Montrose reminded me on the legendary 1990, my first 100pts. wine.

IMG_60293024×4032 2.6 MB

posts.name: Brent Keene
posts.cooked: When you say almost no room for improvement, what do you mean exactly? There’s no benefit to aging the wine?

posts.name: Jürgen Schmidt
posts.cooked:
Yes, no real benefit of BIG aging.

The 2022s already show a great harmony&balance. No harsh tannins, instead the tannins are highly polished. No hard edges, no disturbing acidity and everyting is wonderfully integrated.

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
Robert.A.Jr:

It’s a silly argument because they are not mutually exclusive. I agree that many of these young wines are delicious. But I also agree that they will be more delicious when they finally mature and show the tertiary characteristics that we love in Bordeaux, at least I hope these wines go there.

I couldn’t have put it better or more succinctly.

Like so many things, it comes down to personal preference. There are many who love to drink Bordeaux on release and say it then goes to sleep within a year or two. It doesn’t do that much for me personally, though I may try one on arrival. I’ll usually do my next check-in at 10+ years.

If I’m trying to decide whether to buy more at current pricing, I’ll pop one on release.

posts.name: Jürgen Schmidt

Again, of course they will benefit from aging, like more depth, complexity, structure etc.

BUT I cherish the fact that we don`t have to wait 20-30 years for these „boys&girls“ to come around. Instead maybe (!) in 10 years they already show all the things we are looking for in a great Bordeaux. Hopefully, as I am not 20 anymore.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: People drank the 1929s in the 1930s, the 1953s in the 1950s… Saintsbury was drinking 1864s in the 1870s… In the 1980s, Penning Rowsell in the fifth edition of his Bordeaux book wrote about the 1961s as if they were fully mature and liable to decline. So I think this idea that you need two decades minimum to appreciate Bordeaux is rather aberrant in the longer culture of wine connoisseurship. Certainly, a lot of wines in the second half the 20th century were very austere, with unripe tannins, lacking in fruit/floral aromas due to oxidative handling and/or brettanomyces and/or low levels of anisoles in the winery. With time, those wines have mellowed to become more appealing. But to me that has more to do with production methods than the terroirs and grape varieties.

posts.name: Brent Keene
posts.cooked:
My only point was that my limited experience with young Bordeaux has been disappointing at best. The tannins have been very off-putting to my tastes This was a new phenomenon to my personal experience and I was just surprised by how smooth and balanced everything was at this stage, particularly with a wine of this stature. I’ve typically avoided the region in a large part because it’s such a time investment - so I’ve missed out on this stylistic shift I suppose.

And I’m not condoning drinking all your stash now, but it was still an amazing experience to have a bottle. It was a learning opportunity for me and quite tasty. Will be fun to compare over time rather than consuming them all 25-35 years from now or whatever (and who knows I might not even be here then).

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

People drank the 1929s in the 1930s, the 1953s in the 1950s… Saintsbury was drinking 1864s in the 1870s… In the 1980s, Penning Rowsell in the fifth edition of his Bordeaux book wrote about the 1961s as if they were fully mature and liable to decline. So I think this idea that you need two decades minimum to appreciate Bordeaux is rather aberrant in the longer culture of wine connoisseurship. Certainly, a lot of wines in the second half the 20th century were very austere, with unripe tannins, lacking in fruit/floral aromas due to oxidative handling and/or brettanomyces and/or low levels of anisoles in the winery. With time, those wines have mellowed to become more appealing. But to me that has more to do with production methods than the terroirs and grape varieties.

A few years ago, when I visited the chateau, I was told Latour was using the ‘28 in the fifties as a house wine because they were afraid it would never come round.

There is a financial incentive not to hold wines for long periods of time, and you can see that from wine lists at some of the storied Michelin starred restaurants. This has been the case as long as I have been going to France, starting in the mid eighties. When you do find older wines, there is a massive premium, which essentially made them unaffordable.

You can of course drink the wines whenever you want, but the glory of Bordeaux lies in the complexity that comes with age. Softening of tannins makes for a more pleasant drink as well, and although modern winemaking has made the tannins softer and more approachable, I wonder if it at the expense of longevity and the brilliance of a long aged Bordeaux.

People drank the 1929s in the 1930s, the 1953s in the 1950s… Saintsbury was drinking 1864s in the 1870s… In the 1980s, Penning Rowsell in the fifth edition of his Bordeaux book wrote about the 1961s as if they were fully mature and liable to decline. So I think this idea that you need two decades minimum to appreciate Bordeaux is rather aberrant in the longer culture of wine connoisseurship. Certainly, a lot of wines in the second half the 20th century were very austere, with unripe tannins, lacking in fruit/floral aromas due to oxidative handling and/or brettanomyces and/or low levels of anisoles in the winery. With time, those wines have mellowed to become more appealing. But to me that has more to do with production methods than the terroirs and grape varieties.

I’ve had some amazing 1950s Bordeaux at Bern’s, full-on tertiary. I’m betting they are better when I had them than in the 1950s. But of course, I was not born yet…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I think one trap that people fall into when discussing the aging of wine is extrapolating from the fact that the e.g. 1949 Châteaux X is amazing today that the e.g. 2000 Châteaux X must some day become just as amazing if only you wait long enough. In reality so much has changed: the clonal and agrochemical revolution of the 1970s, yields, winemaking practices and aesthetic ambitions, the climate… (To say nothing of varietal composition or even the plots of land making up the estate itself). Waiting for the hypothetical 2000 in this thought experiment to “come around” may be akin to someone who loves espresso saying that the iced latté they have been served will be great when it eventually warms up. If you read how people described the great pre-modern (if we can call them that) Bordeaux when those wines were young, it is clear that vintages such as 1929 were not short on complexity in their youth. As for 1928s, they are in many cases still taut and angular. They are much easier to find than 1929s, however, because nobody wanted to drink them—for much the same reason that 1870 is one of the most easily found 19th-century vintages.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I’m guessing that most people on this website prognosticating on the trajectory of the 2000s are largely doing so based on trying these wines several times or more since release, it has been 20+ years after all. So they are not necessarily saying the wines will be better at age 50 because the 1961 was, but instead, because they have tried and followed it. If the changes in Bordeaux have been so profound, then projecting forward how they will be in 5, 10, or 25+ years involves a bit of a guess as well. I note that Galloni - not that I am a fan of his Bordeaux notes - has tried this wine several times recently and finds it reticent and closed.

“97+ points, Antonio Galloni for Vinous: “The 2022 Pichon-Longueville Comtesse de Lalande is a wine of somber intensity, one that will require years to be at its best. Dark fruit, leather, incense, licorice, plum and mocha slowly open in the glass. Pichon Comtesse is often more demonstrative at this stage, but the 2022 is decidedly reticent. I expect it will be slow to reveal all of its character. I tasted the 2022 three times and found it consistently closed.” (1/2025)”

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

I think one trap that people fall into when discussing the aging of wine is extrapolating from the fact that the e.g. 1949 Châteaux X is amazing today that the e.g. 2000 Châteaux X must some day become just as amazing if only you wait long enough. In reality so much has changed: the clonal and agrochemical revolution of the 1970s, yields, winemaking practices and aesthetic ambitions, the climate…

Isn’t that extrapolation behind the whole idea of classification system. Wine quality in the past will translate into wine quality in the future.

I take your point that most things from winemaking to climate has changed, but the underlying terroir is still there, and surprisingly, the hierarchy seems relatively intact.

posts.name: Brent Keene
posts.cooked: That note is wild. Crazy how things differ from bottle to bottle and taster to taster.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
There’s quite a lot of stuff out there. Pulling the first thing off my bookshelf that fits, I am dipping into Charles Walter Berry’s In Search of Wine, published in 1935 describing a wine buying journey around France. The Bordeaux chapter opens with Berry drinking a bottle of “1923 Cos d’Estournel … a pleasant enough wine, but much too old for its age”… The 1929 Haut Bailly, he writes a few pages later, will be excellent “in a few years”. Reviewing recent Bordeaux vintages, he classified 1888 and 1914 as years to “drink up quickly” and 1905 and 1907 as years to “drink up slowly”, and says that 1929 is “very good, but I cannot think it will be a vintage to last very long”.

Grabbing a more recent book, one finds Penning Rowsell writing in the early 1980s of the 1961s that “by the late seventies some of them have developed into at least mature wines and in some cases have shown signs of decline”.

Note that both of these texts come out of the British tradition, which favored older wines than the French tended to.

All of this is as much interesting for its salience to the evolving nature of connoisseurship and taste as it is for what is does or doesn’t tell us about the wines.

Overall my personal position (and I write this as someone who loves old wines, with Bordeaux in my cellar back to 1846) is that competently made wines from high-quality vine genetics in great terroirs and good vintages, devoid of masking flaws or coarse extraction, should already be manifestly great in their youth—and that means balanced, complex, characterful and, yes, pleasurable. Aging should be a choice, not an obligation, and one made that the wine may become even better, not in the hope that it may become less objectionable. 2022 seems to me a vintage, much as people described 1929s, that meets all these criteria unusually well.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

There’s quite a lot of stuff out there. Pulling the first thing off my bookshelf that fits, I am dipping into Charles Walter Berry’s In Search of Wine, published in 1935 describing a wine buying journey around France. The Bordeaux chapter opens with Berry drinking a bottle of “1923 Cos d’Estournel … a pleasant enough wine, but much too old for its age”… The 1929 Haut Bailly, he writes a few pages later, will be excellent “in a few years”. Reviewing recent Bordeaux vintages, he classified 1888 and 1914 as years to “drink up quickly” and 1905 and 1907 as years to “drink up slowly”, and says that 1929 is “very good, but I cannot think it will be a vintage to last very long”.

Grabbing a more recent book, one finds Penning Rowsell writing in the early 1980s of the 1961s that “by the late seventies some of them have developed into at least mature wines and in some cases have shown signs of decline”.

Note that both of these texts come out of the British tradition, which favored older wines than the French tended to.

All of this is as much interesting for its salience to the evolving nature of connoisseurship and taste as it is for what is does or doesn’t tell us about the wines.

Overall my personal position (and I write this as someone who loves old wines, with Bordeaux in my cellar back to 1846) is that competently made wines from high-quality vine genetics in great terroirs and good vintages, devoid of masking flaws or coarse extraction, should already be manifestly great in their youth—and that means balanced, complex, characterful and, yes, pleasurable. Aging should be a choice, not an obligation, and one made that the wine may become even better, not in the hope that it may become less objectionable. 2022 seems to me a vintage, much as people described 1929s, that meets all these criteria unusually well.

Personally I like and buy older wines, and tend to buy vintages such as 2016 which I think will age, and I can open in twenty years.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Well said. I agree with caveats. Historically things were quite different. From what I’ve read, and recall hearing from previous generations that tasted now, historic vintages in their youth, years like 21,29, 47, 48, and 49, as well as all the 50s were fabulous on release. But years like 28, 45 and 61 were not charming. This is why you see so many 61s and only a few 59s in cellars and auctions today.

Historically you also need to look at the drinking habits in individual countries. French wine lovers drank them young, and still do. The British preferred aged wines.

Things changed in the 60s with the introduction of a myriad of chemicals to increase yields along with pesticides. Wines were often rustic and charmless.

Today, there is a realization that consumers do not have cellars, patience to wait, or the funds to stockpile wines waiting for maturity.

Climate change, viticultural adaptation, better focus on genetic material, gentler touches from start to finish in the cellars and you have wines that can be thrilling as well as pleasurable in their youth.

2022 is the most recent example balancing high-quality wines of character with the ability to be enjoyed in their youth, yet have the ability to age and evolve for decades.

Those that know me, know I’ve been saying this for years

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
I had the 2022 Comtesse at UGC. I thought it was currently undrinkable but might be excellent in 20+ years, when I will have no interest in it. I found it extremely astringent and lacking in elegance or finesse. I thought that the Baron was much better. The 2022 Comtesse seems to be rather controversial, as at least two other people who tasted it that day felt the same as I did, while some others thought it was the class of the tasting. Personally, my #1 was the Clos Fourtet, which I found currently drinkable and rather elegant. The tannin was not so fierce as to hide the positive aspects of the wine, which was the problem I had with the Comtesse. But reasonable minds may differ.

PS - I thought that the Sauternes were outstanding across the board.

posts.name: S_Yæt€s
posts.cooked: I don’t have a good enough price on this locally yet to try it ($280, no case discount), but did try DDC last week, and whereas “polished tannins, freshness and drinking pleasure” is exactly how I would have described that wine in 2016 on release, in 2022 it was pretty awkward and rough around the edges. It’s interesting how differently these can show at different times in different places.

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked:
Fascinating to read some of those old notes on wines from the late 19th and early 20th centuries. No doubt the changes since then have been enormous.

Earlier accessibility sure seems to be a thing compared to the middle of the 20th century. If you like them young, drink them young. I enjoy them young as long as they’re not shut down. But that aged complexity is a big part of what I love about Bordeaux. It’s worth waiting for.

I think that earlier accessibility and aged complexity can co-exist. Vintages familiar to those of us still enjoying Bordeaux can offer some hope. Many said the 1982s wouldn’t age well: too accessible early, too ripe, too fruit forward, not enough acid. They aged just fine. Same with the 2000s. Not exactly the same as some of my favorites from the ‘80s, but plenty of additional complexity is developing to make it worth cellaring these wines 20-30 years. I’m less sanguine about some of the vintages that pushed ripeness and extraction to the max, but the pendulum has shifted away from those extremes. I have high hopes for 2016 and 2019.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: I opened a 375 of '16 Pichon Comtesse this past week and it was drinking great as a PnP. I did not buy any '22 Pichon, but I did buy '22 Montrose and now I am looking forward to opening a bottle once it is delivered.
